title: Schoolmistress

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Schoolmistress](/w/index.php?title=Schoolmistress&redirect=no "Schoolmistress"))

Term for a male school teacher

For the Haydn symphony, see [Symphony No. 55 (Haydn)](/wiki/Symphony_No._55_(Haydn) "Symphony No. 55 (Haydn)").

For other uses, see [School Master](/wiki/School_Master_(disambiguation) "School Master (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/CoalBarons.JPG/250px-CoalBarons.JPG)](/wiki/File:CoalBarons.JPG)

"The Washington Schoolmaster", a cartoon of 1902 from Chicago

A **schoolmaster**, or simply **master**, is a male [school teacher](/wiki/School_teacher "School teacher"). The usage first occurred in England in the [Late Middle Ages](/wiki/Late_Middle_Ages "Late Middle Ages") and [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period"). At that time, most schools were [one-room](/wiki/One-room_school "One-room school") or [two-room schools](/wiki/Two-room_school "Two-room school") and had only one or two such teachers, a second or third being often called an **assistant schoolmaster**. The use of the traditional term survives in British [private schools](/wiki/Private_schools_in_the_United_Kingdom "Private schools in the United Kingdom"), both [secondary](/wiki/Secondary_school "Secondary school") and [preparatory](/wiki/Preparatory_school_(United_Kingdom) "Preparatory school (United Kingdom)"), and in [grammar schools](/wiki/Grammar_school "Grammar school"), as well as in some Commonwealth boarding schools (such as [the Doon School](/wiki/The_Doon_School "The Doon School") in [India](/wiki/India "India")) which are modelled on British grammar and public schools.

## Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Schoolmaster&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Origins")]

The word "master" in this context translates the [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") word **magister**. In England, a schoolmaster was usually a university graduate, and until the 19th century, the only English universities were [Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") and [Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge"). Their graduates in almost all subjects graduated as [Bachelors of Arts](/wiki/Bachelors_of_Arts "Bachelors of Arts") and were then promoted to [Masters of Arts](/wiki/Master_of_Arts_(Oxford,_Cambridge,_and_Dublin) "Master of Arts (Oxford, Cambridge, and Dublin)") (*magister artium*), simply by seniority. The core subject in an English [grammar school](/wiki/Grammar_school "Grammar school") was Latin.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Present usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Schoolmaster&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Present usage")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Albrecht_D%C3%BCrer_-_Schoolmaster_-_WGA7167.jpg/250px-Albrecht_D%C3%BCrer_-_Schoolmaster_-_WGA7167.jpg)](/wiki/File:Albrecht_D%C3%BCrer_-_Schoolmaster_-_WGA7167.jpg)

Schoolmaster, by [Albrecht Dürer](/wiki/Albrecht_D%C3%BCrer "Albrecht Dürer")

Where a school has more than one schoolmaster, a man in charge of the school is the [headmaster](/wiki/Head_teacher "Head teacher"), sometimes spelt as two words, "head master". This name survives in British independent schools but has been replaced by *head teacher* in most British publicly funded schools, although "headmaster" is often still used colloquially, particularly in grammar schools, and is equivalent to the principal in American schools. The term "headmaster" also survives in some American and [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") independent schools.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

In such schools, a schoolmaster who is not the head is formally called an "assistant schoolmaster".

A range of other terms is derived from "schoolmaster" and "headmaster", including **deputy headmaster** (the second most senior teacher), **senior master** and **second master** (both used in some independent schools instead of deputy headmaster), and [housemaster](/wiki/Housemaster "Housemaster"), the schoolmaster in charge of a [boarding school](/wiki/Boarding_school "Boarding school") [house](/wiki/House_system "House system")).[[3]](#cite_note-3) Some independent schools use other titles for the head of the teaching staff, eg *[High Master](/wiki/High_master_(academic) "High master (academic)")*, *[Rector](/wiki/Rector_(academia) "Rector (academia)")*, *[Warden](/wiki/Warden "Warden")*.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The female equivalent of schoolmaster is *schoolmistress*, which is used with all the same prefixes.[[5]](#cite_note-5) A *[Dame school](/wiki/Dame_school "Dame school")* was taught by a *school dame*, a local woman who would care for young children and teach them the alphabet for a small fee.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Dame schools were localized, and could typically be found at the town or parish level.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Older usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Schoolmaster&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Older usage")]

### Usher

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Schoolmaster&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Usher")]

An older term for the *Second Master* in a school in England was the *Usher*.[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Spelling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Schoolmaster&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Spelling")]

The term schoolmaster was once commonly two words, and the spelling has varied.

[Roger Ascham](/wiki/Roger_Ascham "Roger Ascham")'s book *The Scholemaster Or a Plaine and Perfite Way of Teachyng Children, to Understand, Write and Speake the Latin Tong* was published in 1570.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In 1634, Henry Bury, former master of [Bury Grammar School](/wiki/Bury_Grammar_School "Bury Grammar School"), left in his will £300 to the "ffree school" at Bury "for and towards the yearlie mentayninge of a school maister there, for to teach their children."[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Schoolmaster&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Education in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Education_in_the_United_Kingdom "Education in the United Kingdom")
